title: Do Firms Respond to Gender Pay Gap Transparency?
abstract: We examine the effect of pay transparency on the gender pay gap and firm outcomes. Using a 2006 legislation change in Denmark that requires firms to provide gender-disaggregated wage statistics, detailed employee-employer administrative data, and difference-in-differences and difference-in-discontinuities designs, we find that the law reduces the gender pay gap, primarily by slowing wage growth for male employees. The gender pay gap declines by 2 percentage points, or 13% relative to the prelegislation mean. Despite the reduction of the overall wage bill, the wage transparency mandate does not affect firm profitability, likely because of the offsetting effect of reduced firm productivity.

article.introduction:
Gender pay disparities characterize labormarkets in most developed countries (Goldin (2014), Blau and Kahn (2017)). When a man earns $100, a woman earns $77 in the United States (Goldin (2014)), $78.5 in Germany, $79 in the United Kingdom, and $83.8, on average, across EU countries (Eurostat (2016)). Recent proposals across many countries focus on pay transparency as a mechanism to promote equal pay.1However, evidence on the effect of transparency with respect to gender pay disparities on employee and firm outcomes is limited. In this paper, we draw insights from a regulation in Denmark that increased transparency by requiring companies to inform employees of average wages by gender and occupation.
The consequences of disclosing gender pay gaps are subject to debate. On the one hand, governments often propose transparency as a tool to encourage firms to reduce the pay gap between men and women. Unions and employee groups representing women also tend to believe that secrecy about pay contributes significantly to unequal pay for women.2On the other hand, opponents of pay transparency argue that disclosing gender pay presents a challenge to firms because it lacks practical utility, increases administrative burden, and violates employee privacy.3
The effect of transparency on the gender pay gap and firm outcomes is ultimately an empirical question. Whether transparency provides sufficient incentives for firms to adjust their compensation policies is unclear. Moreover, such wage adjustments may have unintended consequences for firm outcomes such as firm productivity and profitability.
Studying this question empirically requires that we address two key challenges: finding exogenous variation in wage transparency at the firm level and obtaining information on wages and employment at the individual level. With respect to the source of exogenous variation, we exploit a 2006 legislation change in Denmark that requires firms with more than 35 employees to report salary data broken down by gender for employee groups large enough to protect individuals' anonymity. Firms must inform their employees of pay gaps between men and women and explain both the design of the statistics and the wage concept used. With respect to data on wages and employment at the individual level, we use administrative records from the Danish Statistics matched employee-employer data set.
In our research design, we compare employees in firms above the 35-employee threshold to employees in firms below this threshold. Because firm size can influence wage dynamics and firm outcomes, our sample only includes employees in firms in a narrow band around 35 employees. Specifically, we estimate a difference-in-differences (diff-in-diff) model in which treated employees are those working in firms that employ 35 to 50 employees prior to the introduction of the law and control employees are those working in firms with 20 to 34 workers.
In our specifications, we control for a variety of time-varying firm and individual characteristics (age, work experience, and firm size), year fixed effects, individual × firm fixed effects, and firm × year fixed effects. By including individual × firm fixed effects, we control for time-invariant person characteristics, time-invariant firm characteristics, and the match between firms and workers, which allows us to compare thesame employee at the same firmbefore and after the regulation change. By including firm × year fixed effects, we absorb any time-varying shocks at the firm level that may be correlated with wages.
Focusing first on the effect of transparency on firm compensation policies, we find that after the passage of the law, wages of male employees in treated firms increase by 1.7 percentage points less than wages of male employees in control firms. The effect is statistically significant at the 1% level and economically important. By contrast, wages of female employees in treated firms increase by 0.3 percentage points more than wages of female employees in control firms, although this difference is not statistically significant. Examining the dynamic effects, we find no evidence of pretreatment trends, whereas we observe a sharp drop in male wage growth in treated firms in 2006, which increases through the end of our sample in 2008.
Moving to firm-level analysis, we confirm the reduction of the pay gap. Specifically, we find that the average ratio of male to female wages (gender pay gap) declines by 1.9 percentage points in treated firms relative to control firms following passage of the law, which represents a 13% reduction relative to the pretreatment mean. We also find that the average decline is more pronounced at the bottom and middle of the wage distribution.
We obtain similar results when we employ a difference-in-discontinuities (diff-in-disc) design following (Grembi, Nannicini, and Troiano (2016)). This approach takes the difference between the pretreatment and posttreatment discontinuity at 35 employees. As such, diff-in-disc analysis allows us to difference out the effect of any potential preexisting discontinuity at 35 employees. In addition, by limiting attention to the effect at the threshold, this method circumvents concerns under the diff-in-diff method that the control firms might not be a good counterfactual for treated firms. Using this approach, we find that male employee wages grow by 1.1 percentage points less for firms above the threshold, with this difference significant at the 1% level. Consistent with our diff-in-diff analysis, the decline in male wages starts in 2006 and increases gradually until the end of the sample. By contrast, we find a positive but insignificant change in female employee wages following the policy change.
Having documented the effect of the law on employee wages, we next investigate how transparency affects employee reallocation and promotions. We find that treated firms hire more female employees than do control firms. This finding is in line with the view that female employees are more willing to seek employment in firms where the gender pay gap is lower. We also find that male employees are less likely to join treated firms, consistent with passage of wage policies being less attractive for male employees in these firms following passage of the legislation. We do not find that female or male employees are more likely to leave treated firms after the regulatory change. We also find that the law affects promotion decisions in a way that favors female employees. Specifically, we find that women are more likely to be promoted to higher-paid positions within the firm, whereas we do not find any significant change in the promotion probability of male employees.
In additional tests, we examine the implications of gender pay transparency for the firm's wage bill, productivity, and profits. Consistent with our employee-level analysis, we find the average wage per employee is reduced in treated firms relative to control firms. Despite the reduction in average wages, however, we do not find any effect on firm profitability, which could be explained by a decline in labor productivity, as previous research shows that employees who learn that they earn less than their peers become less productive (Akerlof and Yellen (1990), Breza, Kaur, and Shamdasani (2018)). We find a negative effect on productivity in some of our specifications.
Our paper contributes to the literature on the effects of pay transparency. Using a sample of workers in an Indian manufacturing plant, Breza, Kaur, and Shamdasani (2018) show that information on peers' salaries leads to negative feelings, reduces job satisfaction, and negatively affects labor productivity.4Cullen and Perez-Truglia (2017,2018) show that employees have misperceptions about the salaries of their peers and superiors and change their behavior when they learn the true salaries. In the context of mandated pay disclosure in the public sector, Card et al. (2012) use a sample of government employees in California and show that after government employee salaries are published online, aggregate worker satisfaction drops. Our paper is distinct from these studies in that they focus largely on job satisfaction and employee productivity, whereas we focus on wages and firm outcomes.
In a paper on how transparency affects wage setting in organizations, Cullen and Pakzad-Hurson (2019) argue that higher transparency results in more equal but lower wages, which results in higher profits for employers. They test the predictions of their model using data from online markets for low-skill, temporary jobs and an online field experiment. Mas (2017) shows that top earners in municipal jobs experience a drop in wages following the public disclosure of wages, which he argues is due primarily to public aversion to visibly exorbitant salaries. These papers focus on the effect of transparency on the distribution of wages, not on the gender pay gap, as in this paper. In addition, while these papers analyze the wages of temporary employees or public-sector employees, wage setting for regular employment contracts in the private sector is likely different. For example, in the public sector, public pressure and public aversion to high compensation or inequalities might play a larger role than in the private sector.
In a recent paper, Baker et al. (2019) show that disclosing the wages of university faculty in Canada reduced the gender pay gap, with this effect driven primarily by universities where faculty are unionized. In this paper, in contrast, we provide evidence based on private firms.
A related literature examines the effect of information sharing on executive compensation. Shue (2013) finds that the exchange of information through peer interactions affects managerial pay. Using data from the Great Depression, Mas (2016) finds that mandated disclosure of executive compensation led to an increase in average CEO pay relative to other highly paid executives in the firm. More generally, Hermalin and Weisbach (2012) argue that an increase in firm disclosure requirements can affect firm value and CEO compensation. These papers focus on executives. In this paper, we are interested in how transparency affects wage and firm outcomes throughout the organization.
Finally, our paper contributes to a growing literature on gender and organizations that points to biases that women face in the professional workforce. Hospido, Laeven, and Lamo (2020) show that gender differences exist in career progression and promotions in central banking, Egan, Matvos, and Seru (2017) show that female advisers face harsher outcomes following misconduct, although this effect is mitigated in firms with more female executives, Adams and Ragunathan (2017) show that gender barriers tend to discourage women from working in finance, Duchin, Simutin, and Sosyura (2020) show that female division managers are allocated less capital, especially in firms where CEOs grew up in male-dominated families, and Tate and Yang (2015) show that male leadership leads to a less female-friendly firm culture. Our findings suggest that regulatory mandates on pay transparency, as a mechanism to overcome biases against women in the workforce, may be effective in closing the gender pay gap.
The rest of the paper proceeds as follows. SectionIdescribes the law. SectionIIdescribes potential mechanisms through which transparency might affect the gender pay gap. SectionIIIdescribes our data and sample and provides summary statistics. SectionIVpresents the main results on wages. SectionVpresents results on joining, leaving, and promotions, and SectionVIpresents results on firm outcomes. SectionVIIexplores heterogeneity of the effect on employee wages. SectionVIIIconcludes.
article.I. The Law:
On June 9, 2006, Denmark adopted Act no. 562, which requires that firms report gender-disaggregated wage statistics. The goal of the law was “to promote visibility and information about wage differentials.”5The law states that each year an employer with 35 or more employees and at least 10 employees of each gender within an occupation classification code (six-digit DISCO code) shall prepare gender-segregated wage statistics for the purpose of informing employees about pay gaps between men and women in the firm.6These statistics must be made available to employees through employee representatives; they are not required to be made available to the general public. The law also allows employers to substitute gender-based wage statistics with an internal report on equal pay. Such reports must include a description of the conditions that determine wages and establish an action plan for equal pay to be implemented.
During the campaign for the snap election in February 2005, the government promised to introduce a law that would require firms with 35 or more employees to produce gender-disaggregated wage statistics.7This campaign promise was generally viewed as an attempt by the government to cultivate a better standing among female voters.8The campaign promise surprised most observers because the same administration had tabled a similar proposal years earlier.
After winning the election in February, there was little discussion about fulfillment of this promise, but eight months after reelection, in October 2005, the government announced that it would introduce a bill to amend the Equal Pay Act. The Ministry of Economics introduced the bill to Parliament in December 2005. The proposal was adopted on June 2006, and the new provisions came into force in January 2007.
Although firms required to report are those that (i) have a minimum of 35 employees and (ii) have at least 10 employees of each gender within an occupation classification code, in practice, firms disregard the occupational-codes criterion.9This observation is consistent with information we obtained from the Confederation of Danish Employers (DA), the largest employer association, that covers 24,200 employers and about a third of Danish employees in the private sector. According to the DA, 35% of firms that report gender-disaggregated wage statistics through them do not satisfy the second criterion but, all of these firms have more than 35 employees. In addition, this finding is consistent with how the law has been interpreted more widely. For instance, description of the law by the EU and the International Labor Organization (ILO) only mentions that firms above the 35-employee threshold must comply.10In AppendixB, we provide additional details on the timeline and implementation of the law.
article.II. How Might Transparency Affect the Ge:
We now discuss mechanisms through which transparency may affect firms' gender pay gap and worker outcomes. An extensive literature provides evidence of the gender pay gap and explores potential causes. (Goldin (2014) and Blau and Kahn (2017) provide a detailed survey of the literature). A large portion of the gender pay gap can be explained by several observable characteristics such as education, experience, hours worked, occupation or industry, and geographical location, among others. Blau and Kahn (2017) find that 91.6% of the gender pay gap in 2010 in the United States can be attributed to these observable factors. In our empirical analysis, we do not capture changes in the gender pay gap driven by these observable factors, because, as we explain in SectionIV, we control for observable characteristics and exploit within-firm-employee variation.
The gender pay gap might be driven by unobservable factors (the “unexplained” component) such as differences in risk aversion or bargaining power between male and female employees. Below, we discuss some of these potential explanations with respect to our results.
A number of studies propose that differences in bargaining power and willingness to bargain between men and women contribute to the gender pay gap. Hall and Krueger (2012) find that women are roughly half as likely as men to bargain for wages, and Babcock and Laschever (2003) find that women are less informed about the market value of their work than men and are less likely to negotiate. By increasing employees' information about their peers' wages, transparency affects the bargaining process. In a recent paper, Cullen and Pakzad-Hurson (2019) provide a dynamic bargaining model of wage negotiations as a function of transparency. In their model, when transparency is high, firms are reluctant to offer an employee high wages because doing so will affect negotiations with other employees. As a result, overall wages decline and dispersion in wages is reduced.
Employees may bargain harder not only because knowledge of peer salaries is useful in negotiation, but also because they dislike unequal wages. Cullen and Perez-Truglia (2017,2018) show that employees have large misperceptions about their peers' salaries. Under greater transparency, employees who realize that the gender pay gap is higher than they previously thought might increase pressure on firms to reduce it.
Alternatively, managers could be averse to being associated with unequal compensation and as a result, reduce the gender gap when compensation information is revealed. Indeed, the U.K. pay transparency regulation introduced in 2017 was intended to shame firms into action. Although firms in Denmark are not required to make salaries public as in the United Kingdom, the same mechanism may be at work because all employees as well as some external stakeholders learn about firm wages following the regulation change.
It is also possible that firms narrow the gender pay gap after information about wages becomes available to employees because doing so is profit maximizing. Prior literature shows that employees who learn that they earn less than their peers become less productive (Akerlof and Yellen (1990), Breza, Kaur, and Shamdasani (2018)). An optimal response of managers to increased transparency may therefore be to reduce wage discrepancies to avoid a reduction in effort provision (Cullen and Pakzad-Hurson (2019)).
The mechanisms above are all related to transparency affecting wage discrepancies by modifying the information environment. An alternative set of mechanisms relate to the role of the government. For instance, the regulatory change may help reduce the gender pay gap to the extent that firms expect additional legislation related to gender pay equality to arrive in the future. It may also be the case that the regulation change reduces the gender pay gap by increasing firms' exposure to penalties for noncompliance.
article.III. Data and Summary Statistics:
Our main data set comprises the matched employer-employee data set from the Integrated Database for Labor Market Research (IDA database) at Statistics Denmark. In addition to the employer's identification number (CVR) and the employee's identification number (CPR), the IDA data contain detailed information on employee compensation, demographics, and occupation. With respect to compensation, we have information on employees' wages and bonuses. Furthermore, for each employee, we observe their age, gender, and education, as well as their position in the firm hierarchy.
We merge these data with firm-level outcomes from the Danish Business Register. This data set covers all firms incorporated in Denmark and includes the information that these firms are required to file with the Ministry of Economics and Business Affairs, including the value of total assets, number of employees, and revenues. While most firms in this data set are privately held, external accountants audit firm financial information in compliance with Danish corporate law. We link information in the firm-level data set to the matched employer-employee data set using the firm identifier (CVR number).
We start with the universe of limited liability firms in Denmark and their employees included in the IDA data set. For ease of comparison, for the employee-level outcomes, we focus on full-time workers excluding CEOs and directors. We drop firms in industries unaffected by the policy (farming, gardening, forestry, and fishery). We require that firms have nonmissing financial information, which results in 0.8% of firm-years being dropped from the sample. We end up with a panel of employee-firm-years for the period 2003 to 2008 that we use to test whether transparency on wages by gender has real effects on firms' compensation policies.
We perform a difference-in-difference-in-differences analysis we compare the effect of the regulation on male wages (i.e., difference between the change in male wages in treated firms relative to control firms) to its effect on female wages. We define treated firms as firms that employ 35 to 50 employees prior to the introduction of the law, and control firms as those that employ 20 to 34 workers. We focus on a narrow window around the 35-employee cutoff so that control firms are close in size to treated firms and hence likely to be a valid counterfactual. We define treated and control groups based on the 35-employee threshold only, that is, we do not take into account the criterion that treated firms should have at least 10 male and 10 female employees within a six-digit DISCO code, since firms do not typically have DISCO code information, as we discuss in AppendixB.
TableIpresents summary statistics for the treated and control firms in our sample over the 2003 to 2005 period prior to passage of the law on gender pay disclosure. Panel A presents summary information on employee-level characteristics, and Panel B on firm-level characteristics.11The average annual (hourly) wage for employees in treated firms is $54,500 ($34.4), whereas for the control group it is $53,000 ($33.5). The average employee in the sample is 40 years old and has 17 years of work experience in both the treated and the control groups. On average, 25% of employees in the treated and control groups hold a college degree. Consistent with the well-documented employer size-wage effect (Brown and Medoff (1989), Idson and Oi (1999)), the average individual wage in treated firms is higher than that in control firms. However, the average employee is similar in terms of other observable characteristics between treated and control firms.
Treated firms are larger than control firms by construction. For example, as shown in Panel B, the average treated firm has 42 employees pretreatment, assets of $7.2 million, sales of $11.68 million, and total wages of $2.3 million, compared to 26 employees, $5.8 million in assets, $7.73 million in sales, and $1.4 million in wages for control firms. However, the two groups of firms are similar in terms of their pretreatment productivity, cost structure, and gender composition of their employees, with 70% of employees on average being male.
Panel B also shows the “unexplained” part of the prelaw gender pay gap for treated and control firms.12When we do not control for observables, we find a gender pay gap of 18.7% on average prior to the regulation. When we control for observables, we estimate a pretreatment gender pay gap of 15.4% on average, which is not statistically significant between treated and control firms.13
In TableII, we report results of univariate tests that show the main effect of the regulation on wages. We calculate the difference between the average log wage over years 2006 to 2008 and the average log wage over years 2003 to 2005, the three years prior to the introduction of the law. To control for compositional changes, we return only those observations for which the employee works at the same firm as in 2005.
We find that wages increase for all employees, irrespective of their gender, in both the treated and the control group. However, male employee wages grow by 1.65 percentage points less in treated firms than in control firms, with this difference statistically significant at the 1% level. By contrast, we find no significant difference in female wage growth between treated and control firms. These univariate comparisons suggest that the reform requiring pay transparency resulted in 1.96 percentage point lower wage growth for male employees than for female employees.
Another interesting observation is the rate at which the pay gap changes in treated and control firms. In control firms, the wage growth rate of male and female employees is similar. Specifically, the difference is only −0.0012 and statistically insignificant, which implies no change in the pay gap in control firms. In treated firms, however, the growth rate of male employees is 2.08 percentage points lower than that of female employees, with the difference significant at the 1% level, which implies a reduction in the gender pay gap of around 2 percentage points. This reduction is economically meaningful. The level of the “unexplained” pay gap prior to the reform was 15.4% based on the mean salaries of men and women. The pay gap is therefore reduced by about 13% following the introduction of the gender-based disclosure law.
An alternative way to see the effect of the law on wages is to focus on the discontinuity created by the law at 35 employees. For each employee, we construct the change in log wage from 2005 to 2008. We average across all employees in firms with a given employee size. We plot this average separately for male and females in Panels A and B of Figure1. We also fit a third-order polynomial on each side of the discontinuity. We observe a clear discontinuous drop at the 35-employee threshold for male employees but not for female employees. This observation is consistent with the results in TableII. In Panels C and D of the figure, we repeat the analysis over the preregulatory change period (2003 to 2005). As expected, we observe no discontinuity for male or female employees.
article.IV. Effect of Transparency on Wages:
TableIIIreports the results. Column (1) compares the change in the wages of male employees in treated firms relative to control firms. Column (2) repeats this analysis for female employees. We find that wages of male employees in treated firms grow 1.7 percentage points more slowly than wages of male employees in control firms, with the effect statistically significant at the 1% level. The magnitude of this effect is similar to that in our univariate results in TableII. In contrast, we find a positive but insignificant coefficient on female wages in treated firms relative to control firms in column (2). In column (3), we estimate the triple-differences model of equation (1) that compares the effect of the law on wages of male relative to female employees. The triple-difference coefficient shows that male wage growth is 2 percentage points lower than female wage growth, with the effect statistically significant at the 1% level. In column (4), we additionally control for firm × year fixed effects that absorb time-varying changes at the firm level that could be driving our results.19The triple-difference coefficient is not qualitatively affected.
In columns (5) to (8), we repeat our estimation after additionally controlling for individual time-varying characteristics (age and experience) and firm size (proxied by log sales) to account for the well-documented employer size-wage effect whereby larger firms pay higher wages (e.g., Brown and Medoff (1989), Idson and Oi (1999)). Including firm size is important in our setting given that the treated group includes larger firms by construction. At the same time, acknowledging that firm size is likely an endogenous control that could potentially bias our estimates is important. The fact that the estimated coefficients remain virtually unchanged after controlling for firm size is reassuring.
One potential concern with our result is that it could be driven by differential changes in working hours, with men reducing their hours more than women while compensation per hour remains the same. Prior literature (e.g., Card et al. (2012), Cullen and Perez-Truglia (2017), Breza, Kaur, and Shamdasani (2018)) show that employees change the number of hours worked when they receive feedback about their relative salary. To address this concern, in TableIA.IV, Panel A, we replicate TableIIIusing hours worked as the outcome variable. We find a negative and significant effect on hours worked for both male and female employees in treated firms relative to controls. The estimated coefficient for female employees is larger than for males, although the difference is not statistically significant. These results indicate that a reduction in male employees' working hours relative to female employees' working hours does not explain our main findings. In Panel B, we use hourly wages as the dependent variable. We find that hourly wage growth is 1.2 percentage points lower for male employees in treated firms compared to control firms (column (1)), whereas the triple-difference coefficient shows that male wage growth is 1.3 percentage points lower than female wage growth (column (3)).
Another potential concern is that firms may offset the slower growth of male wages by an increase in bonuses. We address this question in TableIA.V, where we estimate the effect of the law on total compensation (wages and bonus). Because this effect only holds for employees who receive performance pay, we split the sample into employees who never received a bonus in our sample period and those who did. In the overall sample as well as in the two subsamples, we find that the estimated triple-difference coefficient is negative and large in magnitude.
Using data at the employee level, our analyses show that the regulation results in a significant drop in male employees' wage growth and no effect on females' wages. Taken together, these results imply a reduction in the gender pay gap. We confirm this reduction using the gender pay gap at the firm level. Specifically, TableIVpresents estimates from a diff-in-diff specification in which the dependent variable is the firm-level pay gap measured at different percentiles of the wage distribution. To control for compositional changes in the firm-level analysis, we condition this analysis on individuals who work at the same firm as in 2005 (similar to TableII). In columns (1) and (2), we use the average wages of male and female employees to measure the pay gap. We find a 1.9 percentage point decline in treated firms' gender pay gap following the regulation change, as compared to controls—a similar estimate as in our individual-level analysis. This effect is equivalent to a 13% reduction in the pretreatment “unexplained” gender pay gap.
We next evaluate whether this average effect is driven by a few employees at the top of the wage distribution or is more evenly distributed throughout the organization. To do so, we compute the ratio of male to female wages at the firm level at the 10th, 50th, and 90thpercentiles of the wage distribution. We present the results in columns (3) to (8) of TableIV. When comparing wages by gender at the bottom of the wage distribution, we find a large and significant decline of 3.7 percentage points that is statistically significant at the 1% level. We find a 1.9 percentage point decline, significant at the 5% level, when we consider the gender pay ratio based on median wages. The decline in the gender gap is weaker and not statistically significant when we instead consider gender pay differences at the 90thpercentile of the wage distribution; although, the estimated magnitude of a 1.4 percentage point decline is still economically large. Overall, this analysis suggests the regulation affected all levels of the organization but is particularly strong toward the bottom and middle of the wage distribution.
A first-order concern with the diff-in-diff analysis is whether wages follow differential trends across small and large firms. Differential trends alone, however, would not explain our results because our estimated effect on wages is concentrated on male employees (as opposed to all employees). To drive our findings, an omitted variable would need to be correlated with size, and also differentially affect male and female wages. To explore this possibility, we analyze the dynamics of male and female wages.
Figure2, Panel A shows year-by-year coefficient estimates for male employees between 2003 and 2008, omitting 2005, the year in which the law was announced. We find no significant difference in the evolution of male wages between treated and control groups prior to the adoption of the law. We observe a sharp drop in the wages of male employees in treated firms relative to control firms starting in 2006 (0.9 percentage points, significant at the 1% level), which increasingly persists up to 2008 (1.38 percentage points in 2008, significant at the 1% level). In Figure2, Panel B, we look instead at the year-by-year estimates for female wages and observe no significant changes. Consistent with our previous findings, Figure2, Panel C shows that the triple-difference coefficient between male and female employees is insignificant prior to the introduction of the law, drops in 2006 and 2007 (1.14 and 2.17 percentage points, respectively), and remains at that level in 2008. These results support the identifying assumption that wages in treated and control firms were following parallel trends prior to the regulatory change and that the effects only appear after the law was introduced.
A different concern is that some other factor (e.g., another law) differentially affected the wages of men and women in large and small firms around the same time as the disclosure law. In this case, we would still observe parallel trends, but our main results could be potentially explained by this factor rather than the disclosure law we are studying. To address this concern, we perform placebo tests where the treatment threshold takes each of the values between 15 and 100 employees, excluding the range of 20 to 50 employees. We plot the average placebo coefficients in Figure2, Panels B, D, and F, together with our baseline coefficient estimates shown in Panels A, C, and E. We show that the average placebo coefficients are close to zero both before and after the law change in all cases, mitigating concerns that our results are driven by a different factor that affected wages around the same time as introduction of this law because this factor would need to affect firms exactly at the 35-employee cutoff.
A remaining concern with our diff-in-diff analysis is whether the control group, which by definition includes smaller firms, is a good counterfactual. To address this concern, we follow Grembi, Nannicini, and Troiano (2016) and employ a diff-in-disc design.
TableVpresents the main diff-in-disc results. In column (1), we show that the law has a negative effect on wage growth for male employees of firms above the 35-employee cutoff. Male employee wages grow by 1.1 percentage points less for firms above the threshold, with this difference significant at the 1% level. By contrast, a positive but insignificant change is recorded for female employee wages following the policy change, as shown in column (2).
Figure3presents the coefficients for male and female employee wages in year-by-year diff-in-disc regressions. The coefficients are estimates of the change in the discontinuity around the 35-employee threshold between a given year and the base year (2005). In Panel A, which corresponds to male employee wages, no significant change occurs in the discontinuity in years 2003 to 2005, while the discontinuity jumps downward in 2006 and widens gradually over time (−1.2 percentage points in 2006, −1.5 percentage points in 2007, and −1.8 percentage points in 2008). In Panel B, which corresponds to female employees, we show no significant change in the discontinuity in any year in our sample.
We present several robustness checks for our diff-in-disc analysis. First, we test whether firms manipulate employee size to avoid having to comply with the law. In FigureIA.1, we show that the null hypothesis of continuity of the density around 35 employees cannot be rejected either before or after the policy change, suggesting that firms do not systematically manipulate their size. Second, in Internet Appendix TableIA.VII, we test for the balancing of covariates at both the individual level (age, education, gender, work experience, and number of children) and the firm level (female share, profits (in $), assets (in $), and sales (in $)) in 2005. None of these characteristics displays a statistically significant jump around the threshold in 2005. Third, we check the sensitivity of the diff-in-disc estimates to the bandwidth chosen. The results in TableVuse a bandwidth of 65 (so that we estimate equation (2) using firms with 0 to 100 employees). In FigureIA.2, we show that the estimates are very stable along a wide range of bandwidth choices.23
article.V. Joining, Leaving, and Promotions:
Our results establish that the introduction of the gender pay disclosure law has an effect on wages. However, this effect may not be firms' only response. Changes in the way similar employees of different genders are compensated may affect firms' willingness to hire employees of different genders. Moreover, revelation of a pay gap may affect the propensity of employees to seek employment in firms with different gender pay gaps. These responses would result in differences in hiring or departure rates. The mandate for fairer practices may also have spillover effects on other firm decisions, such as employee promotions. Accordingly, we next examine the effect of the law's passage on each of these outcomes.
We start by separately examining the effect of the law on the joining and leaving rates for female and male employees. To this end, we estimate a diff-in-diff model at the firm level with the outcome variable defined as the percentage of female (male) employees joining the firm over total employment and the percentage of female (male) employees leaving the firm over total employment. We present the results in TableVIin a specification with firm and year fixed effects. In Panel A, we find a positive and statistically significant coefficient (at the 1% level) on female employees joining, but an insignificant effect on female employees leaving the treated firms. The magnitudes of the estimates are large. The preregulatory average change of female employees' hiring share is 5%. Our estimates indicate that the law causes female hiring rates to increase by 0.8 percentage points.24One possible interpretation of these results is that firms are able to attract more female employees because they offer relatively more fair compensation.
By contrast, in Panel B we find a negative and significant effect on male employees joining the firm, consistent with the fact that male wage growth in treated firms is lower following introduction of the law, and thus these firms are less attractive to male employees. The results are significant at the 10% level and economically large. Relative to the pretreatment average male hiring share of 8.5%, male hiring rates decrease by 0.8 percentage points. We continue to find an insignificant effect on male employees' departures.
In TableA.II, we present year-by-year coefficient estimates for all four dependent variables. Across all specifications, we find no significant pretrends prior to the legislation. In addition, the estimated coefficients on joining rates for female employees (Panel A) and male employees (Panel B) in treated firms become significant in 2007. We find no significant effects on employees leaving around the regulation change.25
These results highlight the fact that the composition of employees changes in treated firms following the regulation. An interesting question is whether firms are “gaming” the system by hiring more highly paid women and/or firing more highly paid men to report smaller gender pay gaps. We explore this possibility in TableIA.VIIIand find no support for this hypothesis.
We next examine whether the law affected firm promotion decisions. We define a promotion to be any transition from a lower- to a higher-paying occupation code, where we rank occupation codes based on average wages computed in 2002. TableVIIpresents the results. Column (1) shows that male employees are less likely to be promoted to higher-paid positions within the organization, although the effect is not statistically significant. Column (2) shows, in contrast, that female employees in treated firms are more likely to be promoted after that passage of the law than are female employees in control firms. Columns (3) and (4) show that the triple-difference coefficient is statistically significant and economically large with a probability of promotion that is 1.2 to 1.6 percentage points higher for females than for males. Columns (5) to (8) show that the coefficient estimates remain unchanged after controlling for firm size and individual-level controls.
We present robustness tests for the promotion results in the Appendix. TableA.IVreports diff-in-diff year-by-year estimates, consistent with no pretrends prior to the law but a significant differential effect in 2007.26Overall, these results indicate that the regulatory change also improved female employees' ability to climb up the corporate ladder.
Given that we observe more promotions for female employees but no changes in the probability of promotions for male employees, we further investigate whether firms expanded the number of senior positions. In Internet Appendix TableIA.IX, we investigate whether the total number of mid and senior-level managerial positions increased after passage of the law. We find a 1 percentage point increase in those positions in treated relative to control firms following introduction of the law, which explains why the law increases the probability of female promotions while the probability of male promotions remains the same.
article.VI. Firm Performance:
In this section, we explore whether the law on gender pay affects firm-level outcomes. Specifically, we examine its effect on the average wage, firm productivity, and profits. We perform diff-in-diff analysis at the firm level using a specification with firm and year fixed effects and report the results in TableVIII.
In columns (1) and (2), we analyze the effect of the law on the average wages of treated firms compared to control firms. Although our main result indicates that the law reduced the growth rate of male wages, the average wage at treated firms may remain constant or even increase due to composition effects (e.g., treated firms may hire high-wage individuals after passage of the law). We confirm that the average wage per employee (log-transformed) decreases by 2.8%. We observe a negative and significant effect only on employee wages and not on other labor costs such as pensions and other social security costs (columns (3) and (4)) because the latter are not directly impacted by the regulation.
In columns (5) and (6), we examine the effects of the law on firm productivity. The effect on productivity is a priori ambiguous. If information on the gender pay gap lowers the job satisfaction of female employees, it may negatively affect their productivity (Akerlof and Yellen (1990)). A similar effect should obtain if male employees are dissatisfied with lower wage growth relative to their peers. However, if increased transparency and firms' responses increase the perception of fairness among employees, productivity may be positively affected. Although the law may differentially affect the productivity of male versus female employees, our estimates capture the average effect of the law on firm productivity because we do not have data on productivity at the individual employee level. We find that, on average, productivity (measured as the log-transformed sales per employee) drops by 2.7% in treated firms as compared to control firms following the regulation, with this reduction statistically significant at the 5% level.
Finally, in columns (7) and (8), we estimate the effect of the law on firm profitability, measured as profits per employee. Because profits can take negative values and thus cannot be log-transformed, we estimate a Poisson regression model (after left-censoring profits at zero).27We find no effect on firm profits, which can be explained by the offsetting effects of lower employee productivity and wages.
In TableA.VI, we present year-by-year coefficient estimates. We find no significant effects in any years prior to the regulatory change across all measures, which provide evidence in favor of the parallel-trends assumption. Consistent with the individual-level analysis presented in Figure2, average employee wages in treated firms start to decline in 2006 and continue to do so until 2008. Interestingly, the drop in average wages precedes the drop in productivity by one year. None of the coefficients on the pension and other labor cost variable and, most importantly, profits, is statistically significant before or after passage of the law.
We present further results using diff-in-disc analysis, which provides evidence on whether firms above the threshold differentially respond to the policy change after the law in terms of average wages, pension and other labor costs, employee productivity, and profits. We present this analysis in TableA.VII. Consistent with the earlier findings, we find a drop in average wages for firms above the threshold postlegislation. Nevertheless, the drop in productivity is much smaller in economic magnitude and not statistically significant. Finally, we confirm the result that profits are unaffected.
article.VII. Heterogeneity in the Effect of Tran:
Having established the effect of transparency on wage setting in firms, we next discuss cases in which we might expect to find a more pronounced reduction in gender pay gaps.
We start by considering the role of preexisting gender pay inequality at the firm. We first estimate the “unexplained” part of the gender pay gap at the firm level over the period 2003 to 2005, following the methodology detailed in SectionIII. We next classify firms into deciles of gender pay gap and we assess whether firms with a higher pretreatment gender pay gap exhibit larger effects.
We present the results in TableA.VIII. In column (1), we find that the drop in the growth rate of male wages is larger in magnitude for firms with a higher pretreatment gender pay gap. Moving from the lowest decile to the highest decile of the pretreatment gender pay gap distribution, we observe a 3.8-percentage-point-larger drop in the wage growth of male employees. By contrast, we find no significant effect of the regulation on female employees across deciles. In column (3), the differential effect is large in magnitude but not statistically significant, but it becomes significant at the 5% level after controlling for firm-year fixed effects. In sum, our results suggest that firms with higher gender pay inequality close the gender gap more aggressively. This finding might be due to the fact that transparency leads to a more pronounced increase in accountability in these firms.
Note that the relation between the pretreatment gap and the strength of the response is a priori ambiguous. For example, firms with managers who have pro-women preferences might have a smaller gender pay gap and at the same time react more strongly to the disclosure regulation.
Another example is the relation between the pretreatment pay gap and the strength of the response varying by employee characteristics. For example, prior literature shows that women face a wage penalty for having children (Bertrand, Goldin, and Katz (2010), Kleven, Landais, and Søgaard (2018)). In TableA.IX, we show that this same characteristic also affects the strength of the reaction to the regulatory change. Specifically, we show that male employees with children experience a lower decline in wages than male employees without children. As a result, the pretreatment pay gap is higher among employees with children, but it does not close as much for this group.
article.VIII. Conclusion:
The gender pay gap has been the subject of debate among academics and policymakers alike. Recently, governments around the world have proposed increased transparency as a tool to nudge firms to reduce the pay gap between men and women. In this paper, we provide the first systematic study of the role of gender-based disclosure on the gender pay gap.
Empirically investigating the effect of gender pay transparency as a mechanism to reduce gender pay discrimination within firms is challenging because doing so requires both exogenous variation in transparency and detailed information on employee wages. We overcome these hurdles by exploiting a 2006 regulation in Denmark that requires certain companies to report gender-based wage statistics. Using detailed employee-firm matched administrative data and employing both a diff-in-diff and a diff-in-disc design, we find evidence of changes in compensation within firms. Specifically, male employees experience slower wage growth than female employees. Moreover, we find that companies subject to the regulation are more likely to hire and promote women. We also find a negative impact on firm productivity but no significant effects on firm profits.
One caveat of our analysis is that our estimates represent the local average treatment effect for firms around the 35-employee cutoff, and hence they do not necessarily apply to larger or smaller firms. The number of firms in the 20-to 50-employee range, however, is significant—around 20% of firms and 16% of employees in the private sector belong to this firm-level employee size range.
Although the regulation that we consider has reduced the gender pay gap, it has also had the unintended consequence of reducing firms' average employee wage. Policymakers should thus weigh the welfare implications when designing regulation to reduce the gender pay gap.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix B: Details of the Gender Pay Di:
This appendix provides more information regarding the gender pay disclosure law. We include a timeline describing the introduction of the law and discuss the law's implementation and compliance. We gathered information from several sources, including the text of the original law, news articles, an evaluation survey conducted by the Ministry of Employment, and a major employer organization. Finally, we verified the information with a Danish law firm that specializes in labor law.
The initial announcement related to the legislation took place during the electoral campaign in February 2005 and was widely reported by news agencies.28Although this announcement only emphasized the 35-employee cutoff, the actual law states that each year an employer with a minimum of 35 employees and at least 10 employees of each gender within an occupation classification code (six-digit DISCO code) must prepare gender-segregated wage statistics for the purpose of informing employees of wage gaps between men and women in the firm. Firms must make such disclosures once per year each year they meet the requirements of the law. The law does not specify the exact wage measure the firm must use (but it does specify that the measure be at the occupation and gender levels). The firm can decide which wage statistic to compute but has to explain in a report how the wage statistic is constructed and has to ensure the wage statistic is transparent to “allow comparison of women's and men's wages in a meaningful way.” Moreover, firms need to consistently use the same wage statistic throughout their report to facilitate comparability.
To obtain information on the wage statistic that firms commonly used in practice as well as on how firms implemented the policy, we gathered information from two sources: the Danish Employer Confederation and an evaluation study that the Ministry for Employment conducted (The Danish National Centre for Social Research report, Holt and Larsen (2011)) in 2009, three years after the law was passed.29The SFI evaluation report was based on a questionnaire survey sent to management and employee representatives in 740 companies that, according to Statistics Denmark, were covered by the law.30We also reached out to one of Denmark's largest employer associations, the Danish Employer Confederation, to obtain more information on what statistic firms typically constructed. According to these sources, the most common statistics were the percentage differences in wages and mean wages for men and women in the same occupation.
The statistics had to be disclosed by gender and occupation. However, companies stated in the SFI evaluation report “that the DISCO codes are difficult to work with.” To get around the issue that the 10-10 requirement may not be satisfied, one survey respondent noted that firms tend to design their own groups: “What they [Human Resources (HR)] do is that, for example, suppose there is a large IT department with 30 people -22 men and 8 women. Since there is not 10 of each, they are grouped with another department (or similar).” Another firm mentioned that it reported each individual's wage statistics (i.e., not following the DISCO group recommendation).
The gender wage statistics are typically made available to both management and employee representatives. These representatives are then responsible for providing the information to employees and giving them an opportunity to express their views on the statistics. Most companies informed representatives and employees through a collaborative committee meeting between leaders and employees (Samarbejds Udvalg/Medbestemmelse og MEdindflydels ) or through the HR department.
Finally, in terms of enforcement, according to the law, “If a company does not meet the legal requirements, the employer can be punishable by a fine. Anyone will be in the position to report to the police if one thinks that an employer does not comply with the law. The police are then responsible to investigate and determine if there are grounds to press charges.” To the best of our knowledge, no company has been fined.31Nevertheless, according to newspaper articles, employer organizations and their legal advisors were threatening lawsuits if firms did not comply with the law.32
